papers.title: Automate Strategy Finding with LLM in Quant Investment
papers.abstract: Framework using LLMs to mine alpha factors from multimodal financial data. Multi-agent evaluation filters by market status. 53% cumulative return on SSE50.

evaluation.strategy_summary: LLMs generate executable alpha factor candidates from numerical data, research papers, and visual charts. Multi-agent ensemble evaluates and filters.
evaluation.trading_rules: Generate alpha factors via LLM prompts, backtest each, select top performers via ensemble voting
evaluation.applicability_notes: Can mine new factors from NSE data using OpenRouter + Claude

papers.title: Increase Alpha: Performance and Risk of an AI-Driven Trading Framework
papers.abstract: Expert-curated features (Technical + Fundamental + Sentiment) into smaller networks. Sharpe 2.54, <3% drawdown, near-zero S&P correlation.

evaluation.strategy_summary: Avoids massive LLMs in favor of expert-curated features fed into smaller, faster networks. Classic indicators done smartly beat fancy models.
evaluation.trading_rules: Multi-horizon forecasting with dynamic stock selection and quarterly rebalancing. Hyperparameter grid search for profit-taker, stop-loss, and max holding period.
evaluation.applicability_notes: Validates our regime engine approach. Add sentiment layer via FinBERT for potential upgrade.

papers.title: Confidence Weighted Mean Reversion (CWMR) — Online Portfolio Selection
papers.abstract: Weights bets based on volatility of prediction using Gaussian distribution. Sharpe 1.74 in backtests against PAMR and other online algos.

evaluation.strategy_summary: Enhanced mean reversion that weights position size by prediction confidence. Uses Gaussian distribution to model uncertainty.
evaluation.trading_rules: Calculate Z-score over 20-period lookback. Enter when Z-score exceeds 1.0. Size position inversely to prediction variance. Exit at mean reversion (Z=0).
evaluation.applicability_notes: Direct upgrade to our RSI Mean Reversion strategy. Add confidence weighting to position sizing.

papers.title: QuantEvolve: Evolutionary Multi-Agent Strategy Discovery
papers.abstract: Evolutionary framework generating diverse strategies via hypothesis-driven agents. Adapts to market regimes with personalized investor preferences.

evaluation.strategy_summary: Uses MAP-Elites style diversity to explore strategy space. Hypothesis-driven agents propose and refine strategies through evolutionary cycles.
evaluation.trading_rules: Auto-generated strategies via LLM agents. Each strategy backtested and scored. Top performers survive and mutate.
evaluation.applicability_notes: Rex could run a lightweight version — evolve strategies weekly using OpenRouter.

papers.title: ML Enhanced Multi-Factor Quant Trading with Bias Correction
papers.abstract: 500-1000 factors from alpha101 extensions + microstructure signals. PyTorch-accelerated. 20% annualized with Sharpe >2.0 on Chinese A-shares.

evaluation.strategy_summary: Cross-sectional neutralization with geometric Brownian motion data augmentation. Processes 500-1000 factors from open-source alpha101 extensions.
evaluation.trading_rules: Multi-factor alpha discovery pipeline. Tensor-based factor computation. Cross-sectional portfolio construction with bias correction.
evaluation.applicability_notes: Factor library can be adapted for NSE large-caps. Start with alpha101 subset.

papers.title: Regime-Adaptive RL for Portfolio Optimization
papers.abstract: Transformer PPO achieves highest Sharpe via regime-aware reward shaping. LSTM variant offers better interpretability with lower complexity.

evaluation.strategy_summary: PPO-LSTM agent trained with regime probabilities as hybrid observations. Volatility penalties and tail-risk shocks in reward function.
evaluation.trading_rules: Agent receives asset returns + hidden regime probabilities. Dynamically reallocates capital. LSTM captures regime persistence.
evaluation.applicability_notes: Validates our HMM regime approach. Could upgrade regime detector to LSTM-based for smoother transitions.

papers.title: ARIMA-HMM Adaptive Trading with Smoothed Probabilities
papers.abstract: 3-regime HMM + ARIMA residuals. Full participation in bull, partial in sideways, withdrawal in bear. 3040% cumulative return.

evaluation.strategy_summary: Smoothed regime probabilities enable graduated position sizing instead of binary regime switches. ARIMA models residuals within each regime.
evaluation.trading_rules: Full position in bull (prob>0.7), half position in sideways, zero in bear. Smoothed probabilities prevent whiplash.
evaluation.applicability_notes: Smoothed probabilities are a direct upgrade to our binary regime detector. High priority implementation.

papers.title: Generating Alpha: Hybrid AI Trading with FinBERT Sentiment
papers.abstract: XGBoost + FinBERT sentiment on 100 S&P stocks. Outperforms across bullish runs, rate tightening, and geopolitical shocks.

evaluation.strategy_summary: XGBoost classifier fed with EMA, MACD, RSI, Bollinger + FinBERT news sentiment scores. Validated across multiple macro regimes.
evaluation.trading_rules: Compute technical indicators + daily sentiment score from financial news. XGBoost predicts next-day direction. Position size by ATR.
evaluation.applicability_notes: Almost identical to our strategy stack. Add FinBERT as 9th signal source. High priority.

papers.title: SPX-VIX Risk Computations Via Perturbed Optimal Transport
papers.abstract: We propose a model independent framework for generating SPX and VIX risk scenarios based on a joint optimal transport calibration of their market smiles. Starting from the entropic martingale optimal transport formulation of Guyon, we introduce a perturbation methodology that computes sensitivities of the calibrated coupling using a Fisher information linearization. This allows risk to be generated without performing a full recalibration after market shocks. We further introduce a dimension reduction method based on perturbed optimal transport that produces fast and stable risk estimates while preserving the structural properties of the calibrated model. The approach is combined with Skew Stickiness Ratio(SSR) dynamics to translate SPX shocks into perturbations of forward variance and VIX distributions. Numerical experiments show that the proposed method produces accurate risk estimates relative to full recalibration while being computationally much faster. A backtesting study also demonstrates improved hedging performance compared with stochastic local volatility models.

evaluation.strategy_summary: This research paper presents a framework for generating SPX and VIX risk scenarios and proposes a hedging strategy using optimal transport and Skew Stickiness Ratio (SSR) dynamics. The core idea is to efficiently translate SPX shocks into VIX distribution perturbations, allowing for faster and more stable risk estimates compared to full recalibration. The improved risk estimates, capturing the joint dynamics of SPX and VIX, are then used to enhance hedging performance compared to Stochastic Local Volatility models.
evaluation.trading_rules:
The paper focuses on hedging and risk management rather than a specific directional trading strategy. The trading rules would revolve around dynamically adjusting hedge positions based on the risk scenarios generated by the proposed method:
1. **Calibrate OT:** Calibrate the entropic martingale optimal transport with SPX and VIX smiles. 
2. **Compute Sensitivities:** Calculate sensitivities of the calibrated coupling using Fisher information linearization for SPX shocks.
3. **Translate SPX Shocks:** Use Skew Stickiness Ratio (SSR) dynamics to translate SPX shocks into perturbations of forward variance and VIX distributions.
4. **Generate Risk Scenarios:** Generate risk scenarios by perturbing the optimal transport formulation based on the shock.
5. **Adjust Hedge Positions:** Adjust hedge positions dynamically based on the generated risk scenarios. This would involve buying or selling SPX options (or futures) and/or VIX futures, attempting to minimize portfolio variance under the updated risk distribution.
evaluation.data_requirements: SPX option prices (smiles/surfaces), VIX option prices (smiles/surfaces), SPX spot prices, VIX spot prices, and potentially historical SPX and VIX prices for SSR modelling. Need to have access to sufficiently liquid option chains for accurate smile construction.
evaluation.applicability_notes: This strategy could be adapted for the Indian market using Nifty (for SPX) and India VIX options. However, the liquidity of India VIX options might be a limiting factor compared to the more liquid VIX options market. The SSR parameters would need to be recalibrated for the Nifty and India VIX relationship. Backtesting on historical Nifty and India VIX data would be essential. Requires careful consideration of market microstructure and trading costs on NSE/BSE.

papers.title: Hybrid Hidden Markov Model for Modeling Equity Excess Growth Rate Dynamics: A Discrete-State Approach with Jump-Diffusion
papers.abstract: Generating synthetic financial time series that preserve statistical properties of real market data is essential for stress testing, risk model validation, and scenario design. Existing approaches, from parametric models to deep generative networks, struggle to simultaneously reproduce heavy-tailed distributions, negligible linear autocorrelation, and persistent volatility clustering. We propose a hybrid hidden Markov framework that discretizes continuous excess growth rates into Laplace quantile-defined market states and augments regime switching with a Poisson-driven jump-duration mechanism to enforce realistic tail-state dwell times. Parameters are estimated by direct transition counting, bypassing the Baum-Welch EM algorithm. Synthetic data quality is evaluated using Kolmogorov-Smirnov and Anderson-Darling pass rates for distributional fidelity, and ACF mean absolute error for temporal structure. Applied to ten years of SPY data across 1,000 simulated paths, the framework achieves KS and AD pass rates exceeding 97% and 91% in-sample and 94% out-of-sample (calendar year 2025), partially reproducing the ARCH effect that standard regime-switching models miss. No single model dominates all quality dimensions: GARCH(1,1) reproduces volatility clustering more accurately but fails distributional tests (5.5% KS pass rate), while the standard HMM without jumps achieves higher distributional fidelity but cannot generate persistent high-volatility regimes. The proposed framework offers the best joint quality profile across distributional, temporal, and tail-coverage metrics. A Single-Index Model extension propagates the SPY factor path to a 424-asset universe, enabling scalable correlated synthetic path generation while preserving cross-sectional correlation structure.

evaluation.strategy_summary: This paper focuses on generating synthetic financial time series that mimic real market data, especially concerning heavy-tailed distributions, negligible linear autocorrelation, and persistent volatility clustering. While the paper itself doesn't explicitly describe a trading strategy, inferentially, one could build a strategy that capitalizes on the identified market regimes using the Hidden Markov Model augmented with a Poisson-driven jump-duration mechanism.
evaluation.trading_rules:
The core of the potential strategy would involve these rules:
1. **Regime Identification:** Implement the Hybrid Hidden Markov Model described in the paper to identify the current market regime (based on discretized excess growth rates of an index like SPY).
2. **Regime Definition:** Define specific trading actions for each identified market regime. For example:
    *   **High-Growth, Low Volatility Regime:** Slightly bullish, long position in SPY (or an ETF tracking a similar index).
    *   **Low-Growth, High Volatility Regime (Jump State implied):** Short SPY (or an ETF tracking a similar index), or implement volatility-based strategies (e.g., buying VIX calls).
    *   **Other Regimes:** Neutral position, or regime-specific strategies based on refined characterization.
3.  **Dynamic Position Sizing:** Adjust position size based on the confidence level of the regime identification and the historical performance of the associated strategy in the identified regime.
4. **Transition Management:** Implement risk management rules (stop-loss orders) to limit losses during regime transitions.
evaluation.data_requirements: Historical time series data (e.g., daily or intraday) for an index similar to SPY (S&P 500) - Ideally, OHLCV data is needed to calculate the excess growth rate which is central to the model. Potentially VIX data as a volatility indicator.
evaluation.applicability_notes: The model can be adapted to the Indian market by using NIFTY 50 data instead of SPY data. The excess growth rate will be calculated based on NIFTY 50 returns. Regime switching strategies such as those used with SPY can be replicated using equivalent NIFTY 50 ETF instruments.

papers.title: Competition between DEXs through Dynamic Fees
papers.abstract: We find an approximate Nash equilibrium in a game between decentralized exchanges (DEXs) that compete for order flow by setting dynamic trading fees. We characterize the equilibrium via a coupled system of partial differential equations and derive tractable approximate closed-form expressions for the equilibrium fees. Our analysis shows that the two-regime structure found in monopoly models persists under competition: pools alternate between raising fees to deter arbitrage and lowering fees to attract noise trading and increase volatility. Under competition, however, the switching boundary shifts from the oracle price to a weighted average of the oracle and competitors' exchange rates. Our numerical experiments show that, holding total liquidity fixed, an increase in the number of competing DEXs reduces execution slippage for strategic liquidity takers and lowers fee revenue per DEX. Finally, the effect on noise traders' slippage depends on market activity: they are worse off in low-activity markets but better off in high-activity ones.

evaluation.strategy_summary: This paper analyzes the impact of dynamic fees on Decentralized Exchanges (DEXs) competing for order flow. The implied trading strategy involves identifying periods when a DEX is likely to raise or lower fees based on the oracle price, competitor's exchange rates, and market volatility. A trader could then strategically execute trades on the DEX likely offering the most advantageous fees based on their trading objectives (minimizing slippage for strategic liquidity takers or exploiting volatility). However, directly applying the findings as a profitable trading strategy could be difficult without significant further research and backtesting.
evaluation.trading_rules:
The paper explores a two-regime structure where DEXs either raise fees to deter arbitrage or lower fees to attract noise trading. Therefore, implied rules can be like this:

1.  **Fee Prediction:** Predict whether a given DEX will increase or decrease fees based on the 'switching boundary' (weighted average of oracle and competitor's price) and overall market activity.
2.  **Strategic Liquidity Taker:** If predicting a fee *decrease*, execute a large trade to take advantage of lower slippage. The timing depends on the size of your position relative to liquidity and you would want to be near the boundaries but before everyone else tries to rush in.
3.  **Volatility Exploitation:** If predicting a fee *increase*, potentially profit from short-term volatility, recognizing that this is primarily attractive to noise traders. This may involve short-term trades around the switching period. 
4. **Arbitrage Opprotunity Based on Fee Differences:** Monitor fees charged by different DEXs and the Oracle Price and identify short term arbitrage opportunites among these DEXs.
evaluation.data_requirements:
1.  On-chain DEX data (transaction fees, liquidity pool sizes, trading volume, token prices).
2.  Oracle price data (more robust or secure source).
3.  Competitor DEX exchange rates (real-time price on other DEXs).
4.  Market activity indicators (overall volume, volatility).
evaluation.applicability_notes: The strategy is primarily designed for DEXs, which are not directly applicable to the Indian NSE/BSE. However, the underlying principles of dynamic fee competition might be relevant to understanding high-frequency trading dynamics, order book behavior, and liquidity provision/liquidity taking in the Indian markets. Applying this to NSE/BSE would require a significant translation of concepts from DEX/DeFi to traditional exchange mechanics.

papers.title: AlgoXpert Alpha Research Framework. A Rigorous IS WFA OOS Protocol for Mitigating Overfitting in Quantitative Strategies
papers.abstract: Transitioning a strategy from backtest to live trading is a common failure point for quantitative systems due to parameter overfitting, selection bias, and sensitivity to regime changes. This paper presents the AlgoXpert Alpha Research Framework, a standardized protocol that evaluates strategies across three stages: In Sample (IS), which focuses on stable parameter regions instead of single optima; Walk Forward Analysis (WFA) using rolling windows and purge gaps to reduce information leakage, supported by majority pass and catastrophic veto rules; and Out of Sample (OOS) testing under strict parameter lock with no further tuning.   The framework applies a defense in depth structure that includes structural safeguards such as cliff veto, execution controls such as spread and leverage guards, and equity protection mechanisms such as circuit breakers and a kill switch. A case study on USDJPY M5 intraday data demonstrates how to detect overfitting through performance decay and drawdown behavior across chronological stages. A post validation comparison of four alpha variants (v1 to v4) shows rank reversal when the objective changes from maximizing Sharpe to minimizing maximum drawdown, highlighting the trade off between risk adjusted performance and tail risk control.

evaluation.strategy_summary: The paper focuses on a framework for rigorous backtesting and validation of quantitative trading strategies, rather than a specific strategy prescription. The case study uses USDJPY M5 intraday data to demonstrate the framework, involving four alpha variants (v1-v4). Objectives are explored in relation to risk-adjusted returns measured by Sharpe ratio versus drawdown control, indicating potentially diverse strategies.
evaluation.trading_rules: The paper does *not* define specific buy/sell rules. It analyzes four undetailed 'alpha variants'. Without further access, the rules cannot be extracted. The paper mentions spread and leverage limits which would factor into execution.
evaluation.data_requirements: USDJPY M5 intraday OHLCV data is used in the case study.
evaluation.reported_sharpe: The paper mentions 'maximizing Sharpe,' implying it is tracked but the specific Sharpe ratios are 'not reported'. It highlights rank reversal based on Sharpe.
evaluation.max_drawdown: The paper mentions 'minimizing maximum drawdown,' implying it is tracked but the specific values are 'not reported'. It highlights the tradeoff between Sharpe and maximum drawdown.
evaluation.applicability_notes: The general principles of IS/WFA/OOS would apply to NSE/BSE. However, USDJPY is a currency pair not directly traded on NSE/BSE. Equivalent strategies would need to be developed and tested for relevant Indian assets (stocks, futures & options, currency pairs like USDINR).

papers.title: Differential Machine Learning for 0DTE Options with Stochastic Volatility and Jumps
papers.abstract: We present a differential machine learning method for zero-days-to-expiry (0DTE) options under a stochastic-volatility jump-diffusion model that computes prices and Greeks in a single network evaluation. To handle the ultra-short-maturity regime, we represent the price in Black--Scholes form with a maturity-gated variance correction, and combine supervision on prices and Greeks with a PIDE-residual penalty. To make the jump contribution identifiable, we introduce a separate jump-operator network and train it with a three-stage procedure. In Bates-model simulations, the method improves jump-term approximation relative to one-stage baselines, keeps price errors close to one-stage alternatives while improving Greeks accuracy, produces stable one-day delta hedges, and is substantially faster than a Fourier-based pricing benchmark.

evaluation.strategy_summary: This paper focuses on improving the pricing and hedging of 0DTE (zero-days-to-expiry) options using machine learning under a stochastic-volatility jump-diffusion model. The core strategy involves training a neural network to accurately price 0DTE options and calculate their Greeks (Delta, Gamma, etc.). A delta-hedging strategy is mentioned, using the model's output to adjust positions and mitigate risk.
evaluation.trading_rules:
1. **Price Prediction:** Use the trained differential machine learning model to predict the price of 0DTE options.
2. **Delta Calculation:** Use the model to calculate the Delta of the option.
3. **Delta Hedging:** Continuously adjust the underlying asset position to maintain a delta-neutral portfolio. Specifically, if Delta is positive, sell the underlying asset; if Delta is negative, buy the underlying asset.
4. **Rebalancing Frequency:** Rebalance the portfolio daily (based on 'stable one-day delta hedges'), potentially more frequently if warranted (higher frequency not directly addressed but alluded to by the maturity of the options).
5. **Model Recalibration/Retraining:** Periodically retrain the model with new market data to adapt to changing market conditions.
evaluation.data_requirements: Historical options prices (specifically 0DTE options), the underlying asset price (OHLCV), implied volatility data, any data to characterize jump distribution (potentially implied jump intensities/amplitudes), and potentially proxy data for stochastic volatility (VIX). The stochastic process being modeled is the Bates model which uses stock prices, stochastic volatility and jumps. The paper specifically discusses Bates-model simulations.
evaluation.applicability_notes: The strategy can be applied to the Indian NSE/BSE markets provided that 0DTE options trading is available, and sufficient historical data is accessible to train the machine learning model. The model needs to be trained on market-specific data to capture the unique characteristics of the Indian market. Calibration against the theoretical Bates model can require extensive historical data of related instruments.

papers.title: Weak integrability breaking perturbations in classical integrable models on the lattice
papers.abstract: We show how to systematically construct weak integrability breaking perturbations (WIBs) for classical integrable models on the lattice. These perturbations, which allow quasi-conserved quantities, have mostly been explored in quantum systems, where they are expected to delay the onset of thermalization and diffusive transport to timescales far exceeding those predicted by Fermi's golden rule. However, accessing such long-time dynamics in quantum models is computationally challenging. Classical integrable lattice models offer a complementary setting for probing transport and long-time dynamics under WIBs. In this work, we specialize our general framework to construct several families of WIBs for the Ishimori model, the Toda chain, and the Harmonic Oscillator Chain (HOC). Such constructions can help quantify how WIBs contribute to anomalous transport and serve as a benchmark for thermalization studies in perturbed integrable models. An important example is the Fermi-Pasta-Ulam-Tsingou (FPUT) model: Starting from the HOC, we show that the cubic nonlinearity (the alpha-FPUT interaction) is a genuine WIB perturbation. Using the integrals of motion (IoMs) of the Toda lattice, we explicitly construct corrections to the entire hierarchy of the HOC IoMs, thereby obtaining an infinite tower of quasi-conserved quantities for the alpha-FPUT chain. We further identify the corresponding adiabatic gauge potential (AGP) as a nontrivial trilocal generator in real space, and show that, more generally, any cubic, translationally invariant, momentum-conserving perturbation of the HOC admits such a generator and is therefore a WIB. Together with our transport and AGP-variance studies, our results provide a unified classical framework for weak integrability breaking and for diagnosing anomalous thermalization and transport in nearly integrable Hamiltonian lattice systems.

evaluation.strategy_summary: This paper investigates the dynamics of nearly integrable Hamiltonian lattice systems and identifies Weak Integrability Breaking (WIB) perturbations. While the paper focuses on theoretical physics models (Ishimori, Toda, Harmonic Oscillator Chain, FPUT), a loose analogy *might* be drawn to identifying regimes of persistent, non-mean-reverting trends in financial time series. If a financial market regime could be modeled as a nearly integrable system, a 'WIB' could be interpreted as an external shock or change in dynamics that temporarily disrupts apparent equilibrium, leading to extended trending behavior. However, mapping this to a practical trading strategy is speculative and requires significant assumptions.
evaluation.trading_rules: This paper does not directly suggest a trading strategy. Mapping this paper to a concrete trading rule is highly speculative. One possible, highly abstract, application could be: 1. Identify market 'states' that exhibit properties resembling integrable systems (e.g., stable correlations, predictable oscillations). 2. Detect 'WIB' events - sudden shifts in these properties (e.g., correlation breakdowns, volatility spikes). 3. Based on the nature of the 'WIB' (which requires a *massive* research effort to even approximate from the original paper), apply a strategy that profits from extended trends predicted by the WIB. No rules for buying/selling are provided.
evaluation.data_requirements: Historical market data (price, volume) is needed to define the 'integrable system' and identify 'WIBs'. More sophisticated data like correlations between assets, volatility indices, and potentially some derivative pricing models could be considered as surrogate of the original data needed to measure the adiabatic gauge potential (AGP) variance.
evaluation.applicability_notes: The concepts in this paper are extremely abstract and not directly applicable to the NSE. Even the potential application suggested above is highly speculative and requires a significant amount of research to bridge the gap between theoretical physics models and market dynamics. The computational complexity of implementing even a simplified version would be extremely high.

papers.title: Overreaction as an indicator for momentum in algorithmic trading: A Case of AAPL stocks
papers.abstract: This paper investigates whether short-term market overreactions can be systematically predicted and monetized as momentum signals using high-frequency emotional information and modern machine learning methods. Focusing on Apple Inc. (AAPL), we construct a comprehensive intraday dataset that combines volatility normalized returns with transformer-based emotion features extracted from Twitter messages. Overreactions are defined as extreme return realizations relative to contemporaneous volatility and transaction costs and are modeled as a three-class prediction problem. We evaluate the performance of several nonlinear classifiers, including XGBoost, Random Forests, Deep Neural Networks, and Bidirectional LSTMs, across multiple intraday frequencies (1, 5, 10, and 15 minute data). Model outputs are translated into trading strategies and assessed using risk-adjusted performance measures and formal statistical tests. The results show that machine learning models significantly outperform benchmark overreaction rules at ultra short horizons, while classical behavioral momentum effects dominate at intermediate frequencies, particularly around 10 minutes. Explainability analysis based on SHAP reveals that volatility and negative emotions, especially fear and sadness, play a central role in driving predicted overreactions. Overall, the findings demonstrate that emotion-driven overreactions contain a predictable structure that can be exploited by machine learning models, offering new insights into the behavioral origins of intraday momentum and the interaction between sentiment, volatility, and algorithmic trading.

evaluation.strategy_summary: The paper proposes an intraday trading strategy that identifies short-term market overreactions in AAPL stock based on high-frequency emotional information from Twitter and volatility-normalized returns. Machine learning models, specifically XGBoost, Random Forests, Deep Neural Networks, and Bidirectional LSTMs, are trained to predict overreactions defined as extreme return realizations relative to volatility and transaction costs. The models generate buy/sell signals based on the predicted probability of an overreaction, aiming to capitalize on the subsequent momentum effect. At ultra-short horizons, machine learning models outperform simple overreaction rules, while behavioral momentum strategies are more effective at intermediate frequencies.
evaluation.trading_rules:
1. Calculate volatility-normalized returns for AAPL at 1, 5, 10, and 15-minute intervals.
2. Collect Twitter data and extract emotion features (fear, sadness, etc.) using a transformer-based model.
3. Train a machine learning model (XGBoost, Random Forest, DNN, or Bi-LSTM) to predict overreactions based on volatility-normalized returns and emotion features. Overreactions are defined as returns exceeding a threshold relative to contemporaneous volatility and transaction costs (use a three-class classification: Buy, Sell, Hold based on predicted probability).
4. Generate trading signals: If the model predicts a "Buy" overreaction with a high probability (e.g., > 0.7), buy AAPL. If the model estimates a "Sell" overreaction with a high probability (e.g., > 0.7), sell AAPL.
5. Close positions after a predetermined holding period (e.g., one holding period for the corresponding sampling frequency). Rebalance positions on a set frequency (e.g., every interval if the sampling frequency is 1 minute).
evaluation.data_requirements: High-frequency (1, 5, 10, 15-minute) OHLCV data for AAPL, Twitter data related to AAPL, Transformer-based emotion scores (fear, sadness, etc.) from Twitter data or similar sentiment data.
evaluation.applicability_notes: This strategy could be applied to liquid stocks on the NSE/BSE by replacing AAPL data with that of a large-cap Indian stock (e.g., RELIANCE, HDFC Bank). The relevant emotion features would need to be extracted from Indian social media sources (e.g., Twitter with regional filters, local news articles) and translated into sentiment scores. Transaction cost parameters must be calibrated for the Indian market. Consider using alternative sentiment data sources if Twitter coverage for Indian stocks is insufficient.

papers.title: Quantitative Financial Modeling for Sri Lankan Markets: Approach Combining NLP, Clustering and Time-Series Forecasting
papers.abstract: This research introduces a novel quantitative methodology tailored for quantitative finance applications, enabling banks, stockbrokers, and investors to predict economic regimes and market signals in emerging markets, specifically Sri Lankan stock indices (S&P SL20 and ASPI) by integrating Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) sentiment analysis with macroeconomic indicators and advanced time-series forecasting. Designed to leverage quantitative techniques for enhanced risk assessment, portfolio optimization, and trading strategies in volatile environments, the architecture employs FinBERT, a transformer-based NLP model, to extract sentiment from ESG texts, followed by unsupervised clustering (UMAP/HDBSCAN) to identify 5 latent ESG regimes, validated via PCA. These regimes are mapped to economic conditions using a dense neural network and gradient boosting classifier, achieving 84.04% training and 82.0% validation accuracy. Concurrently, time-series models (SRNN, MLP, LSTM, GRU) forecast daily closing prices, with GRU attaining an R-squared of 0.801 and LSTM delivering 52.78% directional accuracy on intraday data. A strong correlation between S&P SL20 and S&P 500, observed through moving average and volatility trend plots, further bolsters forecasting precision. A rule-based fusion logic merges ESG and time-series outputs for final market signals. By addressing literature gaps that overlook emerging markets and holistic integration, this quant-driven framework combines global correlations and local sentiment analysis to offer scalable, accurate tools for quantitative finance professionals navigating complex markets like Sri Lanka.

evaluation.strategy_summary: The trading strategy combines ESG sentiment analysis from textual data with time-series forecasting of Sri Lankan stock indices. ESG sentiment is extracted using FinBERT, clustered into regimes, and mapped to economic conditions using machine learning. Simultaneously, time-series models forecast daily closing prices. The outputs of the ESG analysis and time-series predictions are fused using rule-based logic to generate final market signals, presumably buy/sell signals.
evaluation.trading_rules:
The extracted trading rules are implied but not explicitly stated. A possible interpretation and rule translation might be:
1. **ESG Regime Identification:** Classify current market into one of the five ESG regimes identified by the UMAP/HDBSCAN clustering model, using the current ESG sentiment indicators.
2. **Economic Condition Assignment:** Map the ESG regime identified in step 1 to a corresponding economic condition using the a dense neural network and gradient boosting classifier.
3. **Price Prediction:** Use a GRU or LSTM model to predict the next day's closing price of the S&P SL20 or ASPI.
4. **S&P 500 Correlation Tracking:** Calculate moving average and volatility trend correlation between S&P SL20 (or a comparable Indian index) and S&P 500 to strengthen forecasting precision. Adjust weighting of ESG and time-series signals based on this correlation.
5. **Signal Fusion:** Combine the economic condition assigned by ESG and the price prediction by a time-series model via a predefined rule.
6. **Buy/Sell Action:** If the combined signal indicates a sufficiently positive outlook (e.g., "Strong Growth" economic condition and predicted upswing), then buy. If the combined signal indicates a sufficiently negative outlook (e.g., "Recession" economic condition and predicted downturn), then sell. Positions can be sized as a percentage of equity based on the output likelihood scores or probability estimates from steps 1 and 3.
evaluation.data_requirements: Historical ESG data (textual data relevant to Sri Lankan companies and the indicators), macroeconomic indicators (GDP, inflation, unemployment), historical OHLCV (Open, High, Low, Close, Volume) data for the S&P SL20 and ASPI, as well as the S&P 500 index.
evaluation.applicability_notes: This strategy could be adapted to the Indian NSE/BSE by replacing Sri Lankan data with equivalent Indian data. This includes: (1) gathering ESG text data relevant to Indian companies (2) using Indian indices like NIFTY 50 or Sensex instead of S&P SL20/ASPI (3) retraining the models with Indian data; FinBERT would still be applicable or further fine-tuned on Indian text, and UMAP/HDBSCAN, NN, GBM, and RNN training follows

papers.title: Sentiment Feedback in Equity Markets: Asymmetries, Retail Heterogeneity, and Structural Calibration
papers.abstract: We study how sentiment shocks propagate through equity returns and investor clientele using four independent proxies with sign-aligned kappa-rho parameters. A structural calibration links a one standard deviation innovation in sentiment to a pricing impact of 1.06 basis points with persistence parameter rho = 0.940, yielding a half-life of 11.2 months. The impulse response peaks around the 12-month horizon, indicating slow-moving amplification. Cross-sectionally, a simple D10-D1 portfolio earns 4.0 basis points per month with Sharpe ratios of 0.18-0.85, consistent with tradable exposure to the sentiment factor. Three regularities emerge: (i) positive sentiment innovations transmit more strongly than negative shocks (amplification asymmetry); (ii) effects are concentrated in retail-tilted and non-optionable stocks (clientele heterogeneity); and (iii) responses are state-dependent across volatility regimes - larger on impact in high-VIX months but more persistent in low-VIX months. Baseline time-series fits are parsimonious (R2 ~ 0.001; 420 monthly observations), yet the calibrated dynamics reconcile modest impact estimates with sizable long-short payoffs. Consistent with Miller (1977), a one standard deviation sentiment shock has 1.72-8.69 basis points larger effects in low-breadth stocks across horizons of 1-12 months, is robust to institutional flows, and exhibits volatility state dependence - larger on impact but less persistent in high-VIX months, smaller on impact but more persistent in low-VIX months.

evaluation.strategy_summary: The trading strategy involves constructing a long-short portfolio based on sentiment shocks. The strategy buys stocks in the decile with the highest positive change in sentiment and sells stocks in the decile with the lowest change in sentiment. The effect is stronger in retail-tilted, non-optionable stocks. Performance is state-dependent on VIX, meaning impact and persistence differ based on volatility regime.
evaluation.trading_rules: 1. Calculate sentiment scores for each stock using one of the four proxies mentioned in the paper. 2. Aggregate stocks into deciles (D1 to D10) based on the change in their sentiment scores. 3. Go long on D10 (highest sentiment change). 4. Go short on D1 (lowest sentiment change). 5. Rebalance monthly. 6. Potentially adjust position sizing or allocation based on VIX level: overweigh the strategy in high VIX (for impact) if short-term gains are prioritized, overweigh in low VIX (for persistence) if long-term gains are prioritized. 7. Focus on retail-tilted and non-optionable stocks.
evaluation.data_requirements: Stock prices (presumably daily), sentiment data (using one or more of the four proxies from the full paper). Data required for each stock to determine retail tilt and optionability. VIX or similar volatility index data.
evaluation.applicability_notes: Applicable to NSE/BSE, but requires establishing reliable sentiment proxies for the Indian market. Availability of retail ownership data and optionability information for Indian stocks is crucial. The success of the strategy hinges heavily on the accuracy and relevance of the sentiment proxies used in the Indian market.

papers.title: Adaptive Alpha Weighting with PPO: Enhancing Prompt-Based LLM-Generated Alphas in Quant Trading
papers.abstract: This paper introduces a reinforcement learning framework that employs Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) to dynamically optimize the weights of multiple large language model (LLM)-generated formulaic alphas for stock trading strategies. Formulaic alphas are mathematically defined trading signals derived from price, volume, sentiment, and other data. Although recent studies have shown that LLMs can generate diverse and effective alphas, a critical challenge lies in how to adaptively integrate them under varying market conditions. To address this gap, we leverage a DeepSeek model to generate fifty alphas for ten stocks, and then use PPO to adjust their weights in real time. Experimental results indicate that the PPO-optimized strategy does not consistently deliver the highest cumulative returns across all stocks, but it achieves comparatively higher Sharpe ratios and smaller maximum drawdowns in most cases. When compared with baseline strategies, including equal-weighted, buy-and-hold, random entry/exit, and momentum approaches, PPO demonstrates more stable risk-adjusted performance. The findings highlight the importance of reinforcement learning in the allocation of alpha weights and show the potential of combining LLM-generated signals with adaptive optimization for robust financial forecasting and trading.

evaluation.strategy_summary: The trading strategy uses a reinforcement learning (PPO) agent to dynamically allocate weights to a portfolio of 50 different stock trading alphas generated by a Large Language Model (LLM), specifically a DeepSeek model. These alphas are formulaic and derived from various market data sources. The PPO agent learns to adjust the weights of these alphas in real-time to optimize risk-adjusted returns, aiming for high Sharpe ratios and low drawdowns instead of solely focusing on maximizing cumulative returns.
evaluation.trading_rules:
1. **Generate Alphas:** Use a DeepSeek LLM (or similar) to generate 50 formulaic alphas for each of the 10 stocks. The formulas should be based on price, volume, sentiment, and possibly other relevant data. 
2. **Calculate Alpha Values:** At each time step (e.g., every trading day), calculate the value of each of the 50 alphas for each stock.
3. **PPO Weighting:** Use a Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) agent to generate weights between 0 and 1 for each of the 50 alphas. The PPO agent takes the alpha values and market conditions as input and learns to optimally allocate weights.
4. **Portfolio Construction:**  For each stock, the final signal is the weighted sum of the alphas (sum of (alpha_i * weight_i)). Use this signal to determine position size (long/short) for each stock. The scaling of the final signal to position size is not specified but is likely linear.
5. **Execution:** Enter positions at the beginning of the next time step based on the signals. The PPO agent will adjust alpha weights at each time step.
6. **Rebalancing:** The PPO agent implicitly provides rebalancing by constantly adjusting the alpha weights, and thus, changes the portfolio composition.
evaluation.data_requirements: Price data (OHLCV), volume data, and sentiment data (if incorporated into the LLM-generated alphas). The precise data types powering the LLM-generated formulas will matter. Lagged data is very likely required. Historical data to train the PPO agent is required.
evaluation.reported_sharpe: It achieves comparatively higher Sharpe ratios
evaluation.max_drawdown: smaller maximum drawdowns in most cases
evaluation.applicability_notes: This strategy can be applied to the Indian NSE/BSE markets, but requires adaptation.  The LLM must be capable of generating alphas relevant to the Indian market, potentially needing fine-tuning on Indian market data. Availability of high-quality sentiment data for Indian markets could be a limitation. Commission and slippage costs in the Indian market must be considered and may require adjustment of the alpha weights or the PPO's reward function. The selected 10 stocks might not be representative of the broader NSE/BSE, so a larger and more diverse selection may be beneficial.

papers.title: Backtesting Sentiment Signals for Trading: Evaluating the Viability of Alpha Generation from Sentiment Analysis
papers.abstract: Sentiment analysis, widely used in product reviews, also impacts financial markets by influencing asset prices through microblogs and news articles. Despite research in sentiment-driven finance, many studies focus on sentence-level classification, overlooking its practical application in trading. This study bridges that gap by evaluating sentiment-based trading strategies for generating positive alpha. We conduct a backtesting analysis using sentiment predictions from three models (two classification and one regression) applied to news articles on Dow Jones 30 stocks, comparing them to the benchmark Buy&Hold strategy. Results show all models produced positive returns, with the regression model achieving the highest return of 50.63% over 28 months, outperforming the benchmark Buy&Hold strategy. This highlights the potential of sentiment in enhancing investment strategies and financial decision-making.

evaluation.strategy_summary: The research paper explores creating trading strategies based on sentiment derived from news articles related to Dow Jones 30 stocks. The core idea is to use sentiment scores to predict future price movements and make buy/sell decisions. Three different sentiment models (two classification and one regression) are employed, and their performance is compared against a Buy&Hold benchmark.
evaluation.trading_rules:
The paper doesn't explicitly mention specific trading rules. However, we can infer that a general framework exists. A potential workable set of rules is:
1. Calculate or obtain sentiment scores for relevant news articles for each stock on a daily basis.
2. Establish a threshold for positive and negative sentiment (e.g., sentiment score > 0.2 indicates positive sentiment, and score < -0.2 indicates negative sentiment) or a time-series of regression predictions representing stock sentiment value.
3. Buy Signal: If the sentiment score exceeds the positive threshold, buy the stock at the opening price the next day.
4. Sell Signal: If the sentiment score falls below the negative threshold, sell the stock at the opening price the next day, or if the sentiment time-series value reaches a certain minima.
5. Alternatively, implement a long-short strategy: Buy stocks with positive sentiment exceeding a threshold and short stocks with negative sentiment below a threshold.
evaluation.data_requirements:
1. Historical news articles related to the companies (Dow Jones 30 in this case).
2. A validated and tested Sentiment Analysis Model trained for Finance/Stock Market context.
3. Historical stock prices (OHLCV - Open, High, Low, Close, Volume) for backtesting.
evaluation.applicability_notes: This strategy can be applied to the Indian NSE/BSE markets by using news articles and sentiment analysis models trained on Indian news sources or adapted from existing models. The main challenge will be obtaining reliable and comprehensive news data and training a suitable sentiment model for the Indian market context. Furthermore, regulatory restrictions on short selling may require modifications to the strategy.

papers.title: Transformers Beyond Order: A Chaos-Markov-Gaussian Framework for Short-Term Sentiment Forecasting of Any Financial OHLC timeseries Data
papers.abstract: Short-term sentiment forecasting in financial markets (e.g., stocks, indices) is challenging due to volatility, non-linearity, and noise in OHLC (Open, High, Low, Close) data. This paper introduces a novel CMG (Chaos-Markov-Gaussian) framework that integrates chaos theory, Markov property, and Gaussian processes to improve prediction accuracy. Chaos theory captures nonlinear dynamics; the Markov chain models regime shifts; Gaussian processes add probabilistic reasoning. We enhance the framework with transformer-based deep learning models to capture temporal patterns efficiently. The CMG Framework is designed for fast, resource-efficient, and accurate forecasting of any financial instrument's OHLC time series. Unlike traditional models that require heavy infrastructure and instrument-specific tuning, CMG reduces overhead and generalizes well. We evaluate the framework on market indices, forecasting sentiment for the next trading day's first quarter. A comparative study against statistical, ML, and DL baselines trained on the same dataset with no feature engineering shows CMG consistently outperforms in accuracy and efficiency, making it valuable for analysts and financial institutions.

evaluation.strategy_summary: The described strategy aims to forecast short-term sentiment (positive or negative) using a Chaos-Markov-Gaussian (CMG) framework enhanced with transformer-based deep learning. This framework predicts the sentiment for the next trading day's first quarter based on the historical OHLC data of the instrument. A buying or selling decision is made based on this sentiment prediction: a positive sentiment suggests a buy (or long) position, while negative sentiment suggests a sell (or short) position.
evaluation.trading_rules:
1. Input historical OHLC data for the financial instrument.
2. Apply the CMG framework and transformer model to predict sentiment (positive/negative) for the first quarter of the next trading day.
3. If the predicted sentiment is positive, open a long position at the open of the next day.
4. If the predicted sentiment is negative, open a short position at the open of the next day.
5. Close the position at the end of the first quarter of the trading day.
evaluation.data_requirements: OHLC data (Open, High, Low, Close) for the financial instrument. No explicit sentiment data is required as the CMG Framework generates the sentiment forecast based on the OHLC data.
evaluation.applicability_notes: This strategy can be applied to the Indian NSE/BSE markets. The OHLC data for liquid stocks and indices are readily available. The model would need to be trained on NSE/BSE data to account for market-specific dynamics and trading hours. The strategy focuses on the first quarter of the trading day which is an active trading period in the Indian markets.

papers.title: Adaptive Regime-Aware Stock Price Prediction Using Autoencoder-Gated Dual Node Transformers with Reinforcement Learning Control
papers.abstract: Stock markets exhibit regime-dependent behavior where prediction models optimized for stable conditions often fail during volatile periods. Existing approaches typically treat all market states uniformly or require manual regime labeling, which is expensive and quickly becomes stale as market dynamics evolve. This paper introduces an adaptive prediction framework that adaptively identifies deviations from normal market conditions and routes data through specialized prediction pathways. The architecture consists of three components: (1) an autoencoder trained on normal market conditions that identifies anomalous regimes through reconstruction error, (2) dual node transformer networks specialized for stable and event-driven market conditions respectively, and (3) a Soft Actor-Critic reinforcement learning controller that adaptively tunes the regime detection threshold and pathway blending weights based on prediction performance feedback. The reinforcement learning component enables the system to learn adaptive regime boundaries, defining anomalies as market states where standard prediction approaches fail. Experiments on 20 S&P 500 stocks spanning 1982 to 2025 demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves 0.68% MAPE for one-day predictions without the reinforcement controller and 0.59% MAPE with the full adaptive system, compared to 0.80% for the baseline integrated node transformer. Directional accuracy reaches 72% with the complete framework. The system maintains robust performance during high-volatility periods, with MAPE below 0.85% when baseline models exceed 1.5%. Ablation studies confirm that each component contributes meaningfully: autoencoder routing accounts for 36% relative MAPE degradation upon removal, followed by the SAC controller at 15% and the dual-path architecture at 7%.

evaluation.strategy_summary: This trading strategy utilizes an adaptive machine learning framework to predict future stock prices and generate buy/sell signals. The core idea is to dynamically identify and respond to changing market regimes (stable vs. volatile) using an autoencoder to detect anomalies, dual node transformers for regime-specific predictions, and a reinforcement learning (RL) controller to optimize regime detection and prediction blending. The RL component tunes the anomaly threshold and blends the outputs of the two transformers based on real-time performance feedback, aiming to improve prediction accuracy, especially during volatile periods, translating into superior trading signals.
evaluation.trading_rules:
1. **Data Input:** Feed historical stock price data into the autoencoder and dual node transformers.
2. **Anomaly Detection:**  Calculate the reconstruction error from the autoencoder. If the error exceeds the dynamically adjusted threshold (determined by the SAC controller), classify the current market state as 'anomalous/event-driven'. Otherwise, classify it as 'stable'.
3. **Prediction:** Generate two stock price predictions: one from the "stable" node transformer and one from the "event-driven" node transformer.
4. **Blending Predictions:** Combine the two predictions by applying weights determined by the SAC controller. Higher anomaly scoring will increase the share of event-driven transformer prediction.
5. **Signal Generation:** If the blended prediction indicates a price increase, generate a buy signal. If the blended prediction indicates a price decrease, generate a sell signal. A simple rule can be introduced to only activate the signal if the prediction of price increase/decrease is above certain threshold percentage.
6. **Execution:** Execute trades based on the generated signals.  The execution size can be adjusted according to risk tolerance and capital.
evaluation.data_requirements: Historical stock price data (OHLCV - Open, High, Low, Close, Volume) is the minimum requirement.  The paper specifically mentions 1982-2025, indicating a long history window is beneficial. Potentially, adding other features or sentiment data could improve anomaly detection.
evaluation.applicability_notes: This strategy is applicable to the Indian NSE/BSE markets. However, the models need to be retrained with NSE/BSE data. The parameters of the autoencoder, node transformers, and reinforcement learning controller might require adjustments to optimize performance for the specific characteristics of the Indian stock market. Backtesting using historical NSE/BSE data is crucial before deploying the strategy in a live trading environment. Consider exchange holidays and intraday trading times.

papers.title: Can Blindfolded LLMs Still Trade? An Anonymization-First Framework for Portfolio Optimization
papers.abstract: For LLM trading agents to be genuinely trustworthy, they must demonstrate understanding of market dynamics rather than exploitation of memorized ticker associations. Building responsible multi-agent systems demands rigorous signal validation: proving that predictions reflect legitimate patterns, not pre-trained recall. We address two sources of spurious performance: memorization bias from ticker-specific pre-training, and survivorship bias from flawed backtesting. Our approach is to blindfold the agents--anonymizing all identifiers--and verify whether meaningful signals persist. BlindTrade anonymizes tickers and company names, and four LLM agents output scores along with reasoning. We construct a GNN graph from reasoning embeddings and trade using PPO-DSR policy. On 2025 YTD (through 2025-08-01), we achieved Sharpe 1.40 +/- 0.22 across 20 seeds and validated signal legitimacy through negative control experiments. To assess robustness beyond a single OOS window, we additionally evaluate an extended period (2024--2025), revealing market-regime dependency: the policy excels in volatile conditions but shows reduced alpha in trending bull markets.

evaluation.strategy_summary: The trading strategy involves anonymizing financial data (tickers and company names) to prevent LLM agents from exploiting pre-trained memorization. Four LLM agents provide scores and reasoning for stock selections. A Graph Neural Network (GNN) is constructed from the reasoning embeddings of the LLMs to represent market relationships. A Proximal Policy Optimization with DSR (deep-symbolic reinforcement) policy is used to determine trading decisions based on the GNN output. The strategy is particularly effective in volatile market conditions, and signal legitimacy is verified with negative control experiments.
evaluation.trading_rules:
1. Anonymize ticker symbols and company names.
2. Obtain scores and reasoning from four LLM agents for a set of assets.
3. Generate embeddings of the reasoning provided by the LLMs.
4. Construct a GNN using the embeddings to model market relationships.
5. Train a PPO-DSR policy using the GNN output as input.
6. Trade based on signals generated by the PPO-DSR policy.
evaluation.data_requirements: Financial data (prices, volume), preferably anonymized ticker and company name data. LLM reasoning outputs represented as embeddings. Data is needed to train GNN and PPO-DSR policy.
evaluation.applicability_notes: The strategy could be applied to the Indian NSE/BSE markets. The anonymization approach might need modifications based on data availability and regulations. The LLMs need to be proficient in understanding the Indian market or be retrained on it. The GNN and PPO-DSR policy training can be adapted to Indian market data and asset specifics.

papers.title: ARTEMIS: A Neuro Symbolic Framework for Economically Constrained Market Dynamics
papers.abstract: Deep learning models in quantitative finance often operate as black boxes, lacking interpretability and failing to incorporate fundamental economic principles such as no-arbitrage constraints. This paper introduces ARTEMIS (Arbitrage-free Representation Through Economic Models and Interpretable Symbolics), a novel neuro-symbolic framework combining a continuous-time Laplace Neural Operator encoder, a neural stochastic differential equation regularised by physics-informed losses, and a differentiable symbolic bottleneck that distils interpretable trading rules. The model enforces economic plausibility via two novel regularisation terms: a Feynman-Kac PDE residual penalising local no-arbitrage violations, and a market price of risk penalty bounding the instantaneous Sharpe ratio. We evaluate ARTEMIS against six strong baselines on four datasets: Jane Street, Optiver, Time-IMM, and DSLOB (a synthetic crash regime). Results demonstrate ARTEMIS achieves state-of-the-art directional accuracy, outperforming all baselines on DSLOB (64.96%) and Time-IMM (96.0%). A comprehensive ablation study confirms each component's contribution: removing the PDE loss reduces directional accuracy from 64.89% to 50.32%. Underperformance on Optiver is attributed to its long sequence length and volatility-focused target. By providing interpretable, economically grounded predictions, ARTEMIS bridges the gap between deep learning's power and the transparency demanded in quantitative finance.

evaluation.strategy_summary: The paper proposes a neuro-symbolic framework called ARTEMIS that aims to predict market direction while incorporating economic principles and providing interpretable trading rules. It combines a Laplace Neural Operator, a neural stochastic differential equation, and a differentiable symbolic bottleneck to learn trading rules from market data, enforced by no-arbitrage and market price of risk regularizations. The trading strategy involves predicting directional price movements and likely implementing trades based on these predictions, potentially filtered or modified by the learned symbolic trading rules to ensure alignment with economic constraints.
evaluation.trading_rules:
Based on the abstract, the trading rules are encoded within the "differentiable symbolic bottleneck". While the *exact* rules are not provided in the abstract, we can infer a framework of rules for implementation:
* **Directional Prediction:** Predict whether the price will go up or down based on the model's output.
* **Thresholds/Filters (likely):** The symbolic bottleneck probably involves learned thresholds and rules that determine whether to execute a trade based on the predicted direction and other market conditions captured by the input data. These conditions could involve price levels, volume, or other features.
* **Economic Filters:** The Feynman-Kac PDE residual and market price of risk penalty likely contribute to the trading rules by limiting arbitrage opportunities and excessive risk-taking. This could be represented by requiring predictions to be a certain distance from creating a risk-free profit opportunity.
* **Example inferred rule (Hypothetical):** "IF model predicts price INCREASE and VOLUME is greater than X and implied volatility < Y, THEN BUY."
evaluation.data_requirements: The paper uses the Jane Street, Optiver, Time-IMM, and DSLOB datasets. The exact data is not specified, but is likely to include features like OHLCV (Open, High, Low, Close, Volume), order book data, and time-series data related to futures and options markets.
evaluation.applicability_notes: The ARTEMIS framework could potentially be applied to the Indian NSE/BSE markets, but adaptations would be required. It heavily depends on the availability of high-frequency or order book data, particularly for option and futures markets. The no-arbitrage constraints defined in the paper would need to be carefully adapted to the specifics of the Indian market regulations and trading dynamics. Further research is needed to assess its portability.

papers.title: Hyper-Adaptive Momentum Dynamics for Native Cubic Portfolio Optimization: Avoiding Quadratization Distortion in Higher-Order Cardinality-Constrained Search
papers.abstract: We study cubic cardinality-constrained portfolio optimization, a higher-order extension of the standard Markowitz formulation where three-way sector co-movement terms augment the quadratic risk-return objective. Classical heuristics like simulated annealing (SA) and tabu search require Rosenberg quadratization of these cubic interactions. This inflates the variable count from n to 5n and introduces penalty terms that substantially distort the augmented search landscape. In contrast, Hyper-Adaptive Momentum Dynamics (HAMD) operates directly on the native higher-order objective using a hybrid pipeline combining continuous Hamiltonian search, exact cardinality-preserving projection, and iterated local search (ILS). On a cubic portfolio benchmark under matched 60-second CPU budgets, HAMD achieves substantially lower decoded native cubic objective values than SA and tabu search, yielding single-seed relative improvements of 87.9%, 71.2%, 59.5%, and 46.9% at n = 200, 300, 500, and 1000. In a detailed three-seed study at n = 200, HAMD attains a median native objective of 195.65 (zero variance), while SA and tabu yield 1208.07. Decoded-feasibility analysis shows SA satisfies all exact cardinality and Rosenberg auxiliary constraints, yet decodes to a native objective 80-88% worse than HAMD, demonstrating a surrogate-distortion effect rather than simple infeasibility. Exact calibration on small instances (n = 20, 25, 30) confirms HAMD finds the provably global optimum in 9/9 trials. These results demonstrate that native higher-order search offers a substantial advantage over quadratized surrogate optimization for constrained cubic portfolio problems.

evaluation.strategy_summary: This research proposes a portfolio optimization technique (Hyper-Adaptive Momentum Dynamics, or HAMD) for creating portfolios that incorporate cubic interactions between assets to better model risk-return relationships. The strategy aims to minimize a cubic objective function subject to cardinality constraints (limiting the number of assets in the portfolio). It seeks to improve upon traditional quadratization methods by directly operating on the cubic objective function, which the paper argues leads to lower objective values and potentially better portfolio performance than strategies relying on quadratized approximations. The core idea is to identify an efficient portfolio composition using a hybrid approach of continuous search, cardinality projection, and local search.
evaluation.trading_rules: The paper focuses on portfolio optimization and does *not* specify concrete trading rules like buy/sell triggers based on price or volume. However, an application of this research involves: 1. Daily Calculation: Calculate the optimal portfolio allocation using the HAMD algorithm with the specified cardinality constraints. 2. Portfolio Rebalancing: Rebalance the existing portfolio to match the calculated optimal allocation.
evaluation.data_requirements: Historical asset prices (at a minimum, daily closing prices) are needed to calculate the covariance matrix and higher-order co-movement terms required for the cubic objective function. In general, time-series return information for a universe of assets is required.
evaluation.applicability_notes: This portfolio optimization technique could be applied to the Indian NSE/BSE markets. The main consideration would be the computational cost of the HAMD algorithm, which scales with the number of assets. The availability of high-quality historical data for a sufficiently large set of Indian stocks would also be crucial for building a robust portfolio. Broker limitations for a low liquidity stocks and availability of APIs for automated rebalancing will be deciding factors.

papers.title: E-TRENDS: Enhanced LSTM Trend Forecasting for Equities
papers.abstract: Trend-following strategies underpin many systematic trading approaches yet struggle under nonstationary and nonlinear market regimes. We propose an LSTM-based framework to forecast next-day trend differences ($Δ_t$) for the top 30 S\&P 500 equities, validated across market cycles (2005--2025). Key contributions include: (i) formal proof of bias-variance reduction via differencing, (ii) exhaustive empirical benchmarks against OLS, Ridge, and Lasso, (iii) portfolio simulations confirming economic gains in terms of overall PNL compared to other models like OLS, Ridge, Lasso or LightGBM Regressor

evaluation.strategy_summary: The research proposes a trend-following strategy based on predicting the next-day trend difference (Δt) of equity prices using an LSTM model. The strategy aims to capitalize on predicted price movements by taking long or short positions based on the direction of the forecasted trend difference. The LSTM model is trained on historical data and compared against other models to demonstrate its ability to generate superior PNL.
evaluation.trading_rules: 1. Train an LSTM model to predict the next-day trend difference (Δt) for each of the top 30 S&P 500 equities.  2. Calculate Δt as the difference between the closing price of today and the closing price of tomorrow.  3. At the end of each trading day, use the trained LSTM model to forecast Δt for the next trading day for each equity. 4. If the predicted Δt is positive, take a long position in that equity. 5. If the predicted Δt is negative, take a short position in that equity. 6. Hold the position for one trading day and close the position at the end of the next trading day's trading.
evaluation.data_requirements: Historical price data (specifically closing prices) for the top 30 S&P 500 equities. The paper validated across market cycles (2005--2025) meaning the data should cover similar time periods. Additional features used in the LSTM (if any) are not clear so likely only price information is required for replication.
evaluation.applicability_notes: This strategy can be applied to the Indian NSE/BSE markets by training the LSTM model on historical data of the NIFTY 50 (instead of S&P 500) or a similar collection of liquid Indian equities (top 30 or 50 by market cap or ADTV). Parameter tuning and model retraining would be required for the Indian market due to the differences in market microstructure and characteristics.

papers.title: Conditioning on a Volatility Proxy Compresses the Apparent Timescale of Collective Market Correlation
papers.abstract: We address the attribution problem for apparent slow collective dynamics: is the observed persistence intrinsic, or inherited from a persistent driver? For the leading eigenvalue fraction $ψ_1=λ_{\max}/N$ of S\&P 500 60-day rolling correlation matrices ($237$ stocks, 2004--2023), a VIX-coupled Ornstein--Uhlenbeck model reduces the effective relaxation time from $298$ to $61$ trading days and improves the fit over bare mean reversion by $Δ$BIC$=109$. On the decomposition sample, an informational residual of $\log(\mathrm{VIX})$ alone retains most of that gain ($Δ$BIC$=78.6$), whereas a mechanical VIX proxy alone does not improve the fit. Autocorrelation-matched placebo fields fail ($Δ$BIC$_{\max}=2.7$), disjoint weekly reconstructions still favor the field-coupled model ($Δ$BIC$=140$--$151$), and six anchored chronological holdouts preserve the out-of-sample advantage. Quiet-regime and field-stripped residual autocorrelation controls show the same collapse of persistence. Stronger hidden-variable extensions remain only partially supported. Within the tested stochastic class, conditioning on the observed VIX proxy absorbs most of the apparent slow dynamics.

evaluation.strategy_summary: The research suggests that the apparent slow dynamics in market correlation (specifically, the leading eigenvalue of the S&P 500 correlation matrix) are largely driven by the VIX. A potential trading strategy involves dynamically adjusting exposure to a basket of stocks (e.g., mimicking the S&P 500) based on the VIX level. When the VIX is high, reducing exposure or hedging might be beneficial, expecting mean reversion towards lower correlation. When VIX is low, increasing exposure could capture periods of higher collective movement.
evaluation.trading_rules:
1. **Calculate the 60-day rolling correlation matrix of a basket of stocks (e.g., S&P 500).**
2. **Calculate the leading eigenvalue (λmax) fraction (ψ1 = λmax/N), where N is the number of stocks.**
3. **Obtain the current VIX level.**
4. **Define thresholds VIX_High and VIX_Low.** These need to be determined through backtesting.
5. **If VIX > VIX_High:** Reduce exposure to the stock basket by a predetermined percentage (e.g., 25-50%).  Consider hedging instead of position reduction.
6. **If VIX < VIX_Low:** Increase exposure to the stock basket up to a predetermined level. Use leverage cautiously.
7. **Rebalance at a fixed frequency (e.g., daily or weekly).**
evaluation.data_requirements: Historical prices of a basket of stocks (e.g., constituent stocks within a major index), historical VIX data.
evaluation.applicability_notes: This strategy can be adapted to the Indian NSE/BSE by using the Nifty 50 or other major Indian indices as the basket of stocks. A suitable volatility index analogous to the VIX (e.g., India VIX) is required. The specific parameters (VIX_High, VIX_Low, exposure adjustments) would need to be re-optimized for the Indian market.

papers.title: Performance-Driven Causal Signal Engineering for Financial Markets under Non-Stationarity
papers.abstract: We introduce a performance-driven framework for constructing strictly causal forward-oriented observables in strongly non-stationary time series. The method combines a robustly normalized composite of heterogeneous indicators with a causally computed derivative component, yielding a local phase-leading effect that is amplified near regime transitions while remaining fully causal. A hysteresis-based decision functional maps the observable into discrete system states, with execution delayed by one step to preserve strict temporal ordering. Adaptation is achieved through a walk-forward scheme, in which model parameters are selected using rolling train--validation windows and subsequently applied out-of-sample. In this setting, the validation segment acts as an internal performance screen rather than as a statistical validation set, and no claims of generalization are inferred from it alone. The framework is evaluated on high-frequency financial time series as an experimentally accessible realization of a non-stationary complex system. Under a controlled zero-cost setting, the resulting dynamics exhibit a pronounced risk-reshaping effect, characterized by smoother trajectories and reduced drawdowns relative to direct exposure, and should be interpreted as an upper bound on achievable performance. These results illustrate how causal signal engineering can generate anticipatory structure in non-stationary systems without relying on non-causal information, explicit horizon labeling, or high-capacity predictive models.

evaluation.strategy_summary: This paper proposes a trading strategy based on constructing causal forward-oriented observables from heterogeneous indicators and a causally computed derivative component. The observable is designed to anticipate regime transitions by amplifying leading effects while maintaining causality. A hysteresis-based decision functional maps the observable to discrete system states, triggering buy/sell signals with a one-step delay. The strategy is adaptive, employing a walk-forward scheme to select parameters using rolling train--validation windows and evaluating out-of-sample performance in a zero-cost setting.
evaluation.trading_rules:
1. **Construct Heterogeneous Indicators:** Identify and select a basket of relevant financial indicators. The paper does not give specific suggestions. Example indicators could include moving averages, volume indicators, volatility measures, order book metrics like VWAP. 
2. **Normalize Indicators:** Robustly normalize the heterogeneous indicators to a common scale. Specific normalization robust approaches not explicitly mentioned. Examples could include z-score normalization or robust scaling techniques (e.g., using median and interquartile range).
3. **Causally Compute Derivative Component:** Calculate the causal derivative of the normalized composite indicator. Define a clear delay parameter to guarantee causality. Possible methods include delayed difference operations or other methods that only use present and past data.
4. **Combine Indicators and Derivative Component:** Combine normalized indicators and causally computed derivative components. Using weighted averaging or a weighted linear combination could work. The weights will be determined during the walk-forward training phase.
5. **Map Observable to System States with Hysteresis:** Apply a hysteresis-based decision functional to the combined observable. The hysteresis band defines thresholds for triggering buy/sell signals, preventing excessive whipsawing. The upper threshold triggers a buy and the lower threshold triggers a sell, with the hysteresis band preventing reversals until the opposite threshold is crossed. Define the hysteresis band width during training.
6. **Generate Buy/Sell Signals:** Generate buy/sell signals based on the system state. When the observable crosses the upper hysteresis threshold, Buy. When the observable crosses the lower hysteresis threshold, Sell. All actions are delayed by one time step to guarantee causality.
7. **Walk-Forward Optimization:** Implement a walk-forward optimization scheme using rolling train-validation windows. During the training phase, optimize parameters, such as indicator weights and/or thresholds, hysteresis thresholds to maximize a performance metric on the validation set. 
8. **Execute Trades Out-of-Sample:** Evaluate the optimized model on an out-of-sample dataset.
evaluation.data_requirements: High-frequency financial time series data (e.g., OHLCV). The specific indicators used in the composite will determine additional data requirements. For example, Level 2 market data (order book) if VWAP is included.
evaluation.max_drawdown: reported a risk-reshaping effect, characterized by smoother trajectories and *reduced* drawdowns
evaluation.applicability_notes: This strategy could be applied to the Indian NSE/BSE markets, especially for liquid stocks and futures. The high-frequency data requirement means that the system needs sufficient liquidity to avoid unwanted execution slippage issues. Careful backtesting and parameter tuning are crucial to adapt the strategy to the specific characteristics of the Indian market. Ensure data quality for Indian markets (correct tick data), and account for transaction costs and STT.

papers.title: Preprocessing noise in finite-size quantum key distribution
papers.abstract: It is known that preprocessing noise may boost quantum key distribution by expanding the range of values of tolerated noise. For BB84, adding trusted noise may allow the generation of secret keys even for qubit error rate (QBER) beyond the 11% threshold in the asymptotic regime. Here we study the effect of preprocessing noise in the finite-size regime where only a limited number of signals are exchanged between Alice and Bob. We compute tight numerical lower bounds in terms of the sandwiched Rényi entropy of order alpha, optimized via a two-step Frank-Wolfe algorithm, in the presence of a trusted flipping probability q. We find that trusted noise improves the key rate only for a finite interval of alpha, from the alpha -> 1 limit up to alpha approx 1.4. By optimizing on the value of alpha, we determine finite-size key rates for different values of the QBER, observing enhancement due to trusted noise both in asymptotic and finite-size regimes. Finally, we determine the maximum tolerable QBER as a function of the block size.

evaluation.strategy_summary: This paper focuses on Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) and noise preprocessing, primarily for secure communications, not financial trading. While the paper explores noise filtering and signal processing, these techniques aren't directly translatable into a financial trading strategy. The core concept revolves around tolerating higher error rates through added noise but not finding market mispricings.
evaluation.trading_rules: No direct trading rules can be extracted. The paper discusses secure information transfer in noisy environments, not market prediction. There are no buy/sell triggers based on market data or price action.
evaluation.data_requirements: This paper is not about market data. It uses concepts from quantum information theory and relies on simulating/analyzing bit flips based on a QBER not related to the market.
evaluation.applicability_notes: This paper has no applicability to the Indian NSE/BSE or any other stock market. Its core concepts relate to quantum cryptography for secure communication and do not concern financial instrument trading.

papers.title: Non-Fermi-liquid behaviour of electrons coupled to gauge phonons
papers.abstract: We identify overdamped gauge phonons as a new microscopic route to non-Fermi-liquid behaviour in Dirac materials. These phonons couple to electronic currents rather than densities, thereby realising a lattice analogue of transverse gauge-field mechanisms without requiring proximity to a quantum critical point. By computing the electronic self-energy with a phonon propagator dressed by electron-phonon interactions, we show that the low-energy behaviour is controlled by the orbital susceptibility chi and a dimensionless damping parameter alpha. In the overdamped regime, alpha >> 1, quasiparticles display strong deviations from Fermi-liquid theory. For chi > 0, Fermi-liquid behaviour persists only in a parametrically narrow infrared window before crossing over to non-Fermi-liquid scaling. For chi < 0, the Fermi-liquid regime is replaced by marginal-Fermi-liquid behaviour at the lowest energies, followed by a crossover to non-Fermi-liquid scaling. These results establish strain- induced gauge phonons as a promising source of anomalous metallic behaviour in systems such as twisted bilayer graphene.
